missions.title: Body Fluids and Circulation
missions.description: Comprehensive analysis of blood, lymph, heart structure, cardiac cycle, ECG, and related disorders.

microtargets.title: Blood, Plasma, and Formed Elements
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter: Body Fluids and Circulation
mustKnowFacts: Plasma is 55% of blood, water is 90-92% of plasma.
Erythrocytes (RBCs) lack a nucleus in most mammals and are biconcave.
Leucocytes are nucleated and divided into granulocytes and agranulocytes.
commonTraps: Confusing serum with plasma (Serum = Plasma - clotting factors).
Forgetting the order of abundance of WBCs (Never Let Monkeys Eat Bananas).
masteryCriteria: Identify types of WBCs and their functions.
Recall normal counts of RBCs, WBCs, and platelets.
pyqPatterns: Questions asking to identify the cell type based on its role in immunity or allergic reactions.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What is the difference between plasma and serum?
expectedAnswerPoints: Serum is plasma without clotting factors

microtargets.title: Blood Groups and Coagulation
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter: Body Fluids and Circulation
mustKnowFacts: ABO grouping is based on antigens A and B on RBCs.
O blood group has no antigens, AB has no antibodies.
Erythroblastosis fetalis occurs when Rh- mother has Rh+ fetus.
Coagulation cascade involves prothrombin -> thrombin, fibrinogen -> fibrin.
commonTraps: Confusing universal donor (O-) and universal recipient (AB+).
Forgetting the role of calcium ions (Ca++) in clotting.
masteryCriteria: Predict compatibility for blood transfusions.
Trace the steps of the coagulation cascade.
pyqPatterns: Case studies on Erythroblastosis fetalis.
Identifying the ion essential for blood clotting.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Which ion plays a very important role in blood clotting?

microtargets.title: Human Heart and Cardiac Cycle
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter: Body Fluids and Circulation
mustKnowFacts: Cardiac output = stroke volume × heart rate.
Normal heart rate ≈ 72/min.
SA node initiates impulse; AV node delays impulse.
First heart sound (lub) is closure of AV valves; second (dup) is closure of semilunar valves.

diagrams.name: Section of human heart
diagrams.provesOrShows: Conducting system of heart
commonLabelTraps: Confusing SA and AV node locations

commonTraps: Thinking ventricles empty completely during systole (end-systolic volume remains).
Confusing the sounds lub and dup with valve opening instead of closing.
masteryCriteria: Calculate cardiac output given stroke volume and heart rate.
Sequence the electrical conduction pathway of the heart.
pyqPatterns: Calculating cardiac output.
Identifying the pacemaker of the heart.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What is the formula for calculating cardiac output?
expectedAnswerPoints: Cardiac output = stroke volume × heart rate

microtargets.title: ECG (Electrocardiograph)
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter: Body Fluids and Circulation
mustKnowFacts: P wave = atrial depolarisation.
QRS = ventricular depolarisation.
T wave = ventricular repolarisation.
NEET trap: ventricular repolarisation is not QRS.

diagrams.name: Standard ECG
diagrams.provesOrShows: Electrical activity of heart

commonTraps: Assuming T wave represents atrial repolarisation (it is obscured by QRS).
NEET trap: ventricular repolarisation is not QRS.
masteryCriteria: Match ECG waves to the corresponding electrical events in the heart.
pyqPatterns: Identifying the electrical event corresponding to the T wave.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What does the T wave represent in a standard ECG?

microtargets.title: Disorders of Circulatory System
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter: Body Fluids and Circulation
mustKnowFacts: High blood pressure is 140/90 or higher.
CAD (atherosclerosis) affects vessels supplying blood to heart muscle.
Angina pectoris: acute chest pain when not enough oxygen reaches heart muscle.
Heart failure: heart not pumping enough blood; different from cardiac arrest (heart stops beating).
commonTraps: Confusing heart failure, cardiac arrest, and heart attack.
masteryCriteria: Distinguish between angina, heart failure, and myocardial infarction.
pyqPatterns: Matching disorders with their descriptions.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What is the difference between heart failure and cardiac arrest?
expectedAnswerPoints: Heart failure is inability to pump sufficient blood, cardiac arrest is when heart stops beating

missions.title: Mastery Module 1: Human respiratory system anatomy and Related Concepts
missions.description: Comprehensive analysis covering human respiratory system anatomy, mechanism of breathing, breathing volumes and advanced applications.

microtargets.title: Human respiratory system anatomy
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter: Human Physiology, Section detailing Human respiratory system anatomy
mustKnowFacts: A thorough understanding of Human respiratory system anatomy is frequently appears in NEET examinations.
Applying the core principles of Human respiratory system anatomy correctly is essential for solving integrated numerical and conceptual problems in Human Physiology.

diagrams.name: Anatomy of human respiratory system anatomy
diagrams.provesOrShows: Tissue arrangement

commonTraps: Misinterpreting the advanced application scenarios of Human respiratory system anatomy during high-pressure exam scenarios.
Failing to recall the exact conditions or sign conventions associated with Human respiratory system anatomy.
masteryCriteria: Can accurately solve numericals or identify biological structures related specifically to Human respiratory system anatomy.
Successfully avoids common pitfalls when Human respiratory system anatomy is integrated with other concepts in Human Physiology.
pyqPatterns: NEET PyQs emphasize the application of Human respiratory system anatomy in tricky assertion-reason or statement-based questions.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Explain an application where using the concept of Human respiratory system anatomy can lead to a common student error.

missions.title: Mastery Module 5: Human Physiology Anatomical features 44 and Related Concepts
missions.description: Comprehensive analysis covering Human Physiology Anatomical features 44, Human Physiology Anatomical features 45, Human Physiology Anatomical features 46 and advanced applications.

missions.title: Digestion and Absorption NEET Core
missions.description: Alimentary canal anatomy, digestive enzymes, absorption through villi, assimilation, egestion, and common digestive disorders.

microtargets.title: Human Digestive System Anatomy
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Digestion and Absorption: Human digestive system
mustKnowFacts: The alimentary canal runs from mouth to anus and includes buccal cavity, pharynx, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, rectum, and anus.
Salivary glands, liver, pancreas, and gastric glands are the major digestive glands emphasized in NCERT.
Bile is produced by the liver, stored in the gall bladder, and helps emulsify fats.

diagrams.name: Human digestive system
diagrams.provesOrShows: Route of food and location of accessory digestive glands
commonLabelTraps: Confusing liver secretion with gall bladder storage

commonTraps: Confusing digestion in the buccal cavity with complete carbohydrate digestion.
Forgetting that bile has no digestive enzyme but still supports fat digestion.
masteryCriteria: Label the alimentary canal and accessory glands correctly.
State the role of liver, gall bladder, pancreas, and salivary glands without mixing them.
pyqPatterns: Diagram-label questions on digestive glands and their secretions.
Statement-based questions distinguishing organ, secretion, and site of action.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Which accessory gland produces bile, and where is bile stored before release?
expectedAnswerPoints: Gall bladder stores and concentrates bile

activeRecallQuestions.question: List the alimentary canal parts in order from mouth to anus.

microtargets.title: Enzymes and Sites of Digestion
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Digestion and Absorption: Digestion of food
mustKnowFacts: Salivary amylase begins starch digestion in the mouth.
Pepsin digests proteins in the acidic stomach; trypsin and chymotrypsin continue protein digestion in the small intestine.
Pancreatic lipase acts on emulsified fats, while bile salts increase the surface area for lipase action.
commonTraps: Saying pepsin acts in the small intestine instead of the stomach.
Treating bile as an enzyme rather than an emulsifying secretion.
masteryCriteria: Match each digestive enzyme with substrate, site, and product.
Explain how bile assists lipid digestion without calling it an enzyme.
pyqPatterns: Match-the-column items on enzyme, substrate, and site.
Assertion-reason questions on pH and enzyme activation.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Name the enzyme that starts protein digestion in the stomach and the inactive form from which it is produced.
Why does bile help in fat digestion even though it is not an enzyme?
expectedAnswerPoints: Emulsification increases surface area for lipase

microtargets.title: Absorption, Assimilation, and Digestive Disorders
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Digestion and Absorption: Absorption of digested products and disorders
mustKnowFacts: Villi and microvilli increase the absorptive surface area of the small intestine.
Fatty acids and glycerol enter lacteals after being packaged into small fat droplets.
Assimilation is the use of absorbed nutrients by body tissues; egestion removes undigested material.

diagrams.name: Villus of small intestine
diagrams.provesOrShows: Separate absorption routes for sugars/amino acids and fats

commonTraps: Confusing absorption with assimilation.
Forgetting that fats use lacteals rather than directly entering blood capillaries first.
masteryCriteria: Describe absorption of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats using the correct route.
Differentiate absorption, assimilation, and egestion in one explanation.
pyqPatterns: Questions asking which digestive product enters lymph first.
Statement-based checks on villi, microvilli, and absorption routes.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Which absorbed nutrient products mainly enter lacteals of intestinal villi?
Differentiate absorption and assimilation in digestion.
expectedAnswerPoints: Absorption moves digested nutrients into blood or lymph
Assimilation is use or storage by tissues

missions.title: Breathing and Exchange of Gases NEET Core
missions.description: Respiratory anatomy, mechanics of breathing, lung volumes, gas exchange, gas transport, regulation, and respiratory disorders.

microtargets.title: Human Respiratory System and Alveolar Exchange
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Breathing and Exchange of Gases: Respiratory organs and exchange of gases
mustKnowFacts: The conducting pathway includes nostrils, nasal chamber, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, and terminal bronchioles.
Alveoli are the primary sites of gas exchange because of thin walls, rich capillary supply, and large surface area.
Gases diffuse across the respiratory membrane according to partial pressure gradients.

diagrams.name: Human respiratory system and alveolus
diagrams.provesOrShows: Path of air and site of gas exchange

commonTraps: Saying gas exchange occurs mainly in bronchi instead of alveoli.
Ignoring partial pressure gradients in diffusion questions.
masteryCriteria: Trace airflow from nostrils to alveoli.
Explain oxygen and carbon dioxide diffusion using partial pressure differences.
pyqPatterns: Diagram-label questions on respiratory pathway.
Assertion-reason questions on diffusion across alveoli.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Why are alveoli efficient sites for gaseous exchange?
Name the principle that drives oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange across alveoli.

microtargets.title: Mechanism of Breathing and Respiratory Volumes
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Breathing and Exchange of Gases: Mechanism of breathing and respiratory volumes
mustKnowFacts: Inspiration occurs when diaphragm and external intercostal muscles contract, increasing thoracic volume and lowering intrapulmonary pressure.
Normal tidal volume is about 500 mL in a healthy adult.
Vital capacity is the maximum volume of air a person can breathe out after a forced inspiration.
commonTraps: Reversing diaphragm movement during inspiration and expiration.
Confusing residual volume with expiratory reserve volume.
masteryCriteria: Predict pressure and volume changes during inspiration and expiration.
Identify tidal volume, IRV, ERV, RV, inspiratory capacity, and vital capacity from definitions.
pyqPatterns: Numerical or definition questions on lung volumes and capacities.
Statement-based questions on diaphragm and intercostal muscles.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What happens to diaphragm position and intrapulmonary pressure during inspiration?
expectedAnswerPoints: Intrapulmonary pressure decreases below atmospheric pressure

activeRecallQuestions.question: What is the approximate normal tidal volume in an adult?

microtargets.title: Gas Transport, Regulation, and Respiratory Disorders
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Breathing and Exchange of Gases: Transport of gases, regulation, and disorders
mustKnowFacts: Most oxygen is transported as oxyhaemoglobin, while a small fraction is dissolved in plasma.
Most carbon dioxide is transported as bicarbonate ions; carbonic anhydrase in RBCs accelerates conversion.
Respiratory rhythm is regulated by medullary and pontine centers and by chemosensitive areas responding mainly to CO2 and H+.

diagrams.name: Oxygen dissociation curve
diagrams.provesOrShows: Relationship between pO2 and haemoglobin saturation
commonLabelTraps: Forgetting that low pH and high CO2 shift unloading toward tissues

commonTraps: Confusing oxygen transport percentage with carbon dioxide transport percentage.
Missing that CO2/H+ changes are stronger respiratory regulators than oxygen under normal conditions.
masteryCriteria: Explain oxyhaemoglobin formation and unloading using the dissociation curve.
State the main forms of carbon dioxide transport and the role of carbonic anhydrase.
pyqPatterns: Bohr effect and oxygen dissociation curve interpretation.
Questions on asthma, emphysema, and occupational respiratory disorders.

activeRecallQuestions.question: In what major form is carbon dioxide transported in blood?
Which enzyme in RBCs speeds up conversion between carbon dioxide and carbonic acid?

missions.title: Body Fluids and Circulation NEET Core
missions.description: Blood composition, blood groups, coagulation, lymph, heart structure, cardiac cycle, ECG, circulation pathways, and disorders.

microtargets.title: Blood Composition, Blood Groups, and Coagulation
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Body Fluids and Circulation: Blood, blood groups, and coagulation
mustKnowFacts: Plasma forms about 55 percent of blood and contains water, proteins, salts, nutrients, gases, and wastes.
ABO grouping depends on RBC surface antigens and plasma antibodies; Rh incompatibility can cause erythroblastosis fetalis.
Coagulation converts fibrinogen to fibrin through a cascade requiring calcium ions.

diagrams.name: Blood smear and ABO grouping table
diagrams.provesOrShows: Formed elements and antigen-antibody basis of blood grouping
commonLabelTraps: Confusing antigen on RBC with antibody in plasma

commonTraps: Calling serum the same as plasma.
Forgetting calcium is required for clotting.
masteryCriteria: Differentiate RBC, WBC, platelets, plasma, and serum.
Predict basic ABO/Rh compatibility from antigens and antibodies.
pyqPatterns: Blood group compatibility and Rh factor scenarios.
Questions on plasma proteins and formed elements.

activeRecallQuestions.question: How is serum different from plasma?
expectedAnswerPoints: Serum is plasma without clotting factors such as fibrinogen

activeRecallQuestions.question: Which ion is essential for blood clotting?

microtargets.title: Human Heart, Cardiac Cycle, and ECG
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Body Fluids and Circulation: Human heart, cardiac cycle, and ECG
mustKnowFacts: SA node is the pacemaker; AV node, bundle of His, and Purkinje fibres conduct impulses to ventricles.
Cardiac output equals stroke volume multiplied by heart rate.
P wave represents atrial depolarisation, QRS complex ventricular depolarisation, and T wave ventricular repolarisation.

diagrams.name: Section of human heart with conducting system
diagrams.provesOrShows: Electrical pathway and chamber-valve layout
commonLabelTraps: Swapping pulmonary artery and pulmonary vein
Calling AV node the pacemaker instead of SA node

commonTraps: Thinking heart sounds are caused by valve opening instead of valve closure.
Confusing QRS complex with ventricular repolarisation.
masteryCriteria: Sequence impulse conduction through nodal tissue.
Interpret P, QRS, and T portions of an ECG.
pyqPatterns: ECG wave identification questions.
Cardiac output and pacemaker questions.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What does the QRS complex of an ECG represent?
Write the formula for cardiac output.
expectedAnswerPoints: Cardiac output = stroke volume x heart rate

microtargets.title: Double Circulation, Lymph, and Circulatory Disorders
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Body Fluids and Circulation: Double circulation, lymph, and disorders
mustKnowFacts: Double circulation separates pulmonary and systemic circuits, keeping oxygenated and deoxygenated blood largely separate.
Lymph drains excess interstitial fluid and transports absorbed fats from intestinal lacteals.
Hypertension, coronary artery disease, angina, and heart failure are NCERT-listed circulatory disorders.

diagrams.name: Double circulation pathway
diagrams.provesOrShows: Pulmonary and systemic circuits in sequence
commonLabelTraps: Forgetting pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood

commonTraps: Assuming all arteries carry oxygenated blood.
Confusing heart failure with cardiac arrest.
masteryCriteria: Trace one complete route through pulmonary and systemic circulation.
State the major functions of lymph and identify common circulatory disorders.
pyqPatterns: Blood-flow sequence questions.
Statement-based questions on pulmonary artery, pulmonary vein, and lymph.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Why is human circulation called double circulation?
expectedAnswerPoints: Blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circuit
Pulmonary and systemic circuits are separate

activeRecallQuestions.question: Name one artery that carries deoxygenated blood in adults.

missions.title: Excretory Products and Elimination NEET Core
missions.description: Excretory organs, nephron structure, urine formation, counter-current concentration, hormonal regulation, dialysis, and disorders.

microtargets.title: Human Excretory System and Nephron Structure
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Excretory Products and Their Elimination: Human excretory system and nephron
mustKnowFacts: Each kidney contains about one million nephrons, the structural and functional units of the kidney.
A nephron has the glomerulus, Bowmans capsule, PCT, loop of Henle, DCT, and collecting duct.
Cortical nephrons have shorter loops, while juxtamedullary nephrons have long loops important for concentrated urine.

diagrams.name: Nephron structure
diagrams.provesOrShows: Sites of filtration, reabsorption, secretion, and concentration
commonLabelTraps: Swapping ascending and descending limb permeability

commonTraps: Calling ureter the urine storage organ instead of urinary bladder.
Mixing PCT and DCT locations/functions.
masteryCriteria: Label all major nephron parts.
State the functional role of each nephron segment at NEET level.
pyqPatterns: Diagram-label questions on nephron.
Statement-based questions on cortical and juxtamedullary nephrons.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Name the structural and functional unit of kidney.
Which nephron part receives filtrate immediately after glomerular filtration?

microtargets.title: Urine Formation and Counter-Current Mechanism
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Excretory Products and Their Elimination: Urine formation and concentration
mustKnowFacts: Urine formation involves glomerular filtration, selective reabsorption, and tubular secretion.
GFR is about 125 mL per minute, or roughly 180 L per day, in a healthy adult.
The loop of Henle and vasa recta create and maintain a medullary osmotic gradient for urine concentration.

diagrams.name: Counter-current mechanism
diagrams.provesOrShows: How opposite flows help maintain the osmotic gradient
commonLabelTraps: Saying both limbs are equally permeable to water

commonTraps: Forgetting that most filtrate is reabsorbed.
Confusing filtration pressure with tubular secretion.
masteryCriteria: Explain filtration, reabsorption, and secretion separately.
Describe why loop of Henle and vasa recta are arranged counter-currently.
pyqPatterns: Numerical recall on GFR.
Questions on water and salt permeability in loop of Henle.

activeRecallQuestions.question: List the three main steps of urine formation.
What approximate volume of filtrate is formed per day from normal GFR?

microtargets.title: Regulation of Kidney Function and Excretory Disorders
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Excretory Products and Their Elimination: Regulation and disorders
mustKnowFacts: JGA activates the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system when GFR falls.
ADH increases water reabsorption in distal nephron and collecting duct, helping produce concentrated urine.
Hemodialysis removes nitrogenous wastes from blood in renal failure using a semipermeable membrane.
commonTraps: Confusing ADH with ANP; ADH conserves water while ANP promotes sodium and water loss.
Calling dialysis a cure for kidney failure instead of supportive filtration.
masteryCriteria: Connect ADH, aldosterone, and ANP to water/salt balance.
Explain the principle of hemodialysis and identify uremia.
pyqPatterns: Hormonal regulation of nephron questions.
Clinical-style questions on renal failure and dialysis.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What is the main effect of ADH on urine formation?
Which apparatus in the kidney helps activate RAAS when GFR decreases?

missions.title: Locomotion and Movement NEET Core
missions.description: Types of movement, skeletal muscle ultrastructure, sliding filament theory, skeleton, joints, and movement disorders.

microtargets.title: Types of Movement and Skeletal Muscle Structure
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Locomotion and Movement: Types of movement and skeletal muscle
mustKnowFacts: Amoeboid, ciliary, and muscular movements are the three NCERT movement types.
Skeletal muscle fibres are striated, voluntary, multinucleate, and organized into myofibrils.
A sarcomere extends from one Z-line to the next and contains actin and myosin filaments.

diagrams.name: Sarcomere structure
diagrams.provesOrShows: Structural basis of striations and contraction
commonLabelTraps: Confusing A band with actin-only region

commonTraps: Saying A band shortens during contraction.
Confusing ciliary movement with amoeboid movement.
masteryCriteria: Match movement types to examples.
Label sarcomere bands and filaments correctly.
pyqPatterns: Sarcomere diagram and band-change questions.
Movement type example questions.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What is the functional contractile unit of skeletal muscle?
Which sarcomere band remains constant during muscle contraction?

microtargets.title: Sliding Filament Mechanism of Muscle Contraction
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Locomotion and Movement: Mechanism of muscle contraction
mustKnowFacts: Calcium ions bind troponin, moving tropomyosin away from myosin-binding sites on actin.
ATP hydrolysis powers myosin head movement during cross-bridge cycling.
During contraction, I band and H zone reduce while A band length stays constant.
commonTraps: Forgetting ATP is needed both for cross-bridge movement and detachment.
Saying filaments shorten instead of sliding past each other.
masteryCriteria: Sequence calcium release, binding-site exposure, cross-bridge formation, power stroke, and relaxation.
Predict band changes during contraction.
pyqPatterns: Assertion-reason questions on calcium and ATP.
Band-change questions in sliding filament theory.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What exposes myosin-binding sites on actin during contraction?
expectedAnswerPoints: Tropomyosin shifts away from binding sites

activeRecallQuestions.question: Do actin and myosin filaments themselves shorten during contraction?

microtargets.title: Skeletal System, Joints, and Musculoskeletal Disorders
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Locomotion and Movement: Skeletal system and disorders
mustKnowFacts: The human skeleton has axial and appendicular divisions with 206 bones in adults.
Synovial joints include ball-and-socket, hinge, pivot, gliding, saddle, and condyloid joints.
Myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy, arthritis, osteoporosis, and gout are high-yield disorders.

diagrams.name: Types of synovial joints
diagrams.provesOrShows: Joint type and movement range
commonLabelTraps: Confusing hinge with pivot joint examples

commonTraps: Mixing osteoporosis with arthritis.
Confusing axial skeleton with appendicular skeleton.
masteryCriteria: Classify major bones into axial or appendicular skeleton.
Match joint types and disorders with defining features.
pyqPatterns: Match joint type to example.
Disorder definition questions.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Give one example of a ball-and-socket joint.
Which disorder is caused by autoimmunity at the neuromuscular junction?

missions.title: Neural Control and Coordination NEET Core
missions.description: Neuron structure, impulse conduction, synaptic transmission, reflex action, brain regions, and sense organs.

microtargets.title: Neuron Structure and Nerve Impulse
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Neural Control and Coordination: Neuron and nerve impulse
mustKnowFacts: A neuron has dendrites, cell body, axon, and axon terminals.
Resting membrane potential is maintained mainly by ionic gradients and selective membrane permeability.
Myelinated axons conduct impulses faster through saltatory conduction at nodes of Ranvier.

diagrams.name: Structure of neuron
diagrams.provesOrShows: Polarity and conduction path in a neuron

commonTraps: Reversing the direction of impulse conduction.
Forgetting that depolarisation involves sodium ion entry.
masteryCriteria: Label neuron parts and trace impulse direction.
Explain resting potential, depolarisation, repolarisation, and saltatory conduction.
pyqPatterns: Neuron-label questions.
Statement questions on ions during action potential.

activeRecallQuestions.question: What is saltatory conduction?
expectedAnswerPoints: Impulse jumping from one node of Ranvier to the next in a myelinated axon

activeRecallQuestions.question: During depolarisation of a neuron, which ion mainly enters the axon?

microtargets.title: Synaptic Transmission and Reflex Action
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Neural Control and Coordination: Synapse and reflex action
mustKnowFacts: Chemical synapses transmit signals using neurotransmitters released into the synaptic cleft.
A reflex arc typically includes receptor, sensory neuron, interneuron, motor neuron, and effector.
Synaptic transmission is one-way because neurotransmitter release occurs from the presynaptic terminal.

diagrams.name: Reflex arc
diagrams.provesOrShows: Fast involuntary response pathway

commonTraps: Saying neurotransmitters are released by the postsynaptic membrane.
Forgetting spinal cord can integrate simple reflexes without conscious brain involvement.
masteryCriteria: Sequence chemical synaptic transmission.
Trace a reflex arc from stimulus to response.
pyqPatterns: Reflex arc diagram labeling.
Chemical versus electrical synapse comparisons.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Why is chemical synaptic transmission usually one-way?
expectedAnswerPoints: Neurotransmitters are released from presynaptic terminal

activeRecallQuestions.question: Name the neuron that carries impulses from receptor to CNS in a reflex arc.

microtargets.title: Brain, Eye, Ear, and Sensory Coordination
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Neural Control and Coordination: CNS and sensory organs
mustKnowFacts: Cerebrum handles higher functions; cerebellum coordinates posture and balance; medulla controls vital involuntary functions.
Retina contains rods for dim light and cones for colour vision.
Cochlea is for hearing, while the vestibular apparatus helps maintain balance.

diagrams.name: Human brain and sensory organs
diagrams.provesOrShows: Functional regions of CNS, eye, and ear

commonTraps: Mixing cerebellum balance with cerebrum intelligence functions.
Saying cones are for dim-light vision instead of rods.
masteryCriteria: Match brain regions with functions.
Differentiate rods/cones and cochlea/vestibular apparatus.
pyqPatterns: Function matching for brain parts.
Eye and ear receptor questions.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Which part of the brain coordinates posture and balance?
Which sensory structure is primarily responsible for hearing?

missions.title: Chemical Coordination and Integration NEET Core
missions.description: Endocrine glands, hormone classes, feedback regulation, mechanisms of hormone action, and endocrine disorders.

microtargets.title: Endocrine Glands and Major Hormones
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chemical Coordination and Integration: Endocrine glands and hormones
mustKnowFacts: Hypothalamus releases regulatory hormones that control anterior pituitary secretion.
Pituitary hormones include GH, TSH, ACTH, LH, FSH, prolactin, oxytocin, and vasopressin/ADH.
Thyroid hormones need iodine; pancreatic islets secrete insulin and glucagon.

diagrams.name: Human endocrine glands
diagrams.provesOrShows: Location and hormone-source mapping
commonLabelTraps: Confusing endocrine pancreas with exocrine pancreas

commonTraps: Calling hypothalamus only neural and not neuroendocrine.
Mixing insulin and glucagon effects on blood glucose.
masteryCriteria: Match major endocrine glands with their hormones.
State the primary action of insulin, glucagon, thyroid hormone, and adrenal hormones.
pyqPatterns: Hormone-source match questions.
Statement-based questions on pituitary and hypothalamus.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Which pancreatic hormone lowers blood glucose level?
Which element is required for synthesis of thyroid hormones?

microtargets.title: Mechanism of Hormone Action and Feedback Control
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chemical Coordination and Integration: Mechanism of hormone action
mustKnowFacts: Peptide hormones usually act through membrane receptors and second messengers such as cAMP.
Steroid and thyroid hormones can act through intracellular receptors to regulate gene expression.
Negative feedback stabilizes hormone levels, such as thyroid hormone feedback on pituitary and hypothalamus.

diagrams.name: Peptide versus steroid hormone action
diagrams.provesOrShows: Different receptor locations and response pathways
commonLabelTraps: Saying all hormones enter the nucleus directly

commonTraps: Treating peptide and steroid hormone mechanisms as identical.
Forgetting feedback loops include hypothalamus, pituitary, and target gland.
masteryCriteria: Compare membrane-receptor and intracellular-receptor hormone action.
Trace one negative feedback loop using thyroid hormone.
pyqPatterns: Second messenger questions.
Feedback regulation assertion-reason questions.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Why do peptide hormones usually need second messengers?
expectedAnswerPoints: They generally do not pass directly through the lipid membrane

activeRecallQuestions.question: What kind of feedback usually controls thyroid hormone levels?

microtargets.title: Endocrine Disorders and Clinical Clues
ncertAnchors: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chemical Coordination and Integration: Hormonal disorders
mustKnowFacts: Diabetes mellitus is linked to deficient insulin secretion or reduced insulin response.
Hypothyroidism can cause simple goitre when iodine deficiency reduces thyroid hormone synthesis.
Excess GH in adults causes acromegaly, while deficiency in childhood causes pituitary dwarfism.
commonTraps: Confusing diabetes mellitus with diabetes insipidus.
Mixing gigantism in children with acromegaly in adults.
masteryCriteria: Match hormone excess or deficiency with named disorders.
Use clinical clues to identify diabetes mellitus, goitre, acromegaly, dwarfism, Addison disease, and Cushing syndrome.
pyqPatterns: Disease-hormone matching questions.
Clinical clue questions on thyroid, pituitary, pancreas, and adrenal disorders.

activeRecallQuestions.question: Which hormone deficiency or ineffective action is central to diabetes mellitus?
Differentiate gigantism and acromegaly by timing of GH excess.
expectedAnswerPoints: Gigantism occurs before epiphyseal closure in children
Acromegaly occurs in adults after epiphyseal closure
